assessment_items.question_content:
The following cladogram shows the evolutionary relationships among five types of birds.

[[☃ image 1]]

According to this cladogram, oystercatchers are more closely related to flamingos than to penguins.

**Which of the following statements provides the best justification for this relationship?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Prum, Richard O., Jacob S. Berv, Alex Dornburg, Daniel J. Field, Jeffrey P. Townsend, Emily Moriarty Lemmon, and Alan R. Lemmon. 2015. “A Comprehensive Phylogeny of Birds (Aves) Using Targeted Next-Generation DNA Sequencing” *Nature* 526:569-73. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature15697.

options.alt: A horizontal phylogenetic tree extending from brackets is shown. The top line of the first bracket extends to the end of the diagram and is labeled mallard. The lower line of the first bracket divides into a second bracket. The bottom line of the second bracket extends to the end of the diagram and is labeled penguin. The top line of the second bracket divides into a third bracket. The top line of the third bracket extends to the end of the diagram and is labeled flamingo. The bottom line of the third bracket divides into a fourth bracket. The top line of the fourth bracket extends to the end of the diagram and is labeled Oystercatcher, and the bottom line of the fourth bracket extends to the end of the diagram and is labeled Silver gull.

choices.content: Oystercatchers share a more recent common ancestor with flamingos than with penguins.
The branch of the cladogram with oystercatchers is closer to the branch with flamingos than to the branch with penguins.
Both oystercatchers and flamingos are less closely related to the outgroup than penguins are.
Oystercatchers share a common ancestor with flamingos but not with penguins.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows a set of derived characters for several species of land plants.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the information in the table, which of the following cladograms best represents the evolutionary relationships among the species?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Species with a *higher* number of shared, derived characters are *more* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more recent past.

Species with a *lower* number of shared, derived characters are *less* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more distant past.
The table tells us that French roses and giant sequoias share the most derived characters of any two species in the table, with four characters in common. So, French roses and giant sequoias are more closely related to each other than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with French roses and giant sequoias forming a group that shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]
The table also tells us that royal ferns share the highest number of characters with the group formed by French roses and giant sequoias, with two characters in common. So, royal ferns are more closely related to French roses and giant sequoias than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with royal ferns forming a group with French roses and giant sequoias. This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that Carolina quillworts share the highest number of characters with the group made up of French roses, giant sequoias, and royal ferns, with one character in common. So, Carolina quillworts are more closely related to these three species than to palm mosses. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with Carolina quillworts forming a group with French roses, giant sequoias, and royal ferns. This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

Palm mosses do not share any of the derived characters with the other species in the table. So, palm mosses are equally related to all the other species and can be placed as the outgroup at the base of the tree.

Based on these relationships, the cladogram that is best supported is the one shown below. To help illustrate why, we’ve labeled the cladogram with the derived characters from the table. A derived character is present only in the lineages descending from the common ancestor that appears after the label.

[[☃ image 1]]

Scientists compared the amino acid sequences of mTOR from five different vertebrate species, and recorded the number of amino acid differences in mTOR between each species pair. hints:
Species with *fewer* sequence differences are *more* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more recent past. 

Species with *more* sequence differences are *less* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more distant past.
The table tells us that humans and olive baboons have the fewest differences in their mTOR sequences of any two species in the table, with just four differences. So, humans and olive baboons are more closely related to each other than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with humans and olive baboons forming a group that shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that dogs have the fewest differences in their mTOR sequence when compared to the group formed by humans and baboons, with 15 differences. So, dogs are more closely related to humans and baboons than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with dogs forming a group with humans and baboons. This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that emperor penguins have the fewest differences in their mTOR sequence when compared to the group made up of humans, baboons, and dogs, with between 114 and 117 differences. So, emperor penguins are more closely related to these three species than to zebrafish. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with emperor penguins forming a group with humans, baboons, and dogs. This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

Zebrafish have the highest number of differences in their mTOR sequence when compared to all the other species in the table with between 265 and 244 differences. So, zebrafish are equally related to all the other species and can be placed as the outgroup at the base of the tree. 

Based on these relationships, the cladogram that is best supported is:

[[☃ image 1]]

Many plants sense and respond to light with the help of the phytochrome A protein. The phytochrome A protein is encoded by the *PhyA* gene. 

Scientists compared the nucleotide sequences of the *PhyA* gene from five different plant species. To do this, the scientists calculated the percentage of nucleotides in the *PhyA* gene that were shared by each pair of species. The results are shown in the table below.

Species with *more* sequence similarities are *more* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more recent past. 

Species with *fewer* sequence similarities are *less* closely related. They will have branched from a common ancestor in the more distant past.
The table tells us that *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri* have the highest percentage of identical nucleotides in their *PhyA* sequences, with 97.3% identical nucleotides. So, *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri* are more closely related to each other than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri* forming a group that shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that *C. rubella* has the highest percentage of identical nucleotides in its *PhyA* sequence when compared to the group formed by *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri*, with about 95.0% identical nucleotides. So, *C. rubella* is more closely related to *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri* than to the other species in the table. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with *C. rubella* forming a group with *A. thaliana* and *A. halleri* . This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The table also tells us that *C. sinensis* has the highest percentage of identical nucleotides in its *PhyA* sequence when compared to the group made up of *A. thaliana*, *A. halleri*, and *C. rubella*, with around 76.0% identical nucleotides. So, *C. sinensis* is more closely related to these three species than to *G. max*. This relationship can be shown in a cladogram with *C. sinensis* forming a group with *A. thaliana*, *A. halleri*, and *C. rubella*. This group shares a most recent common ancestor, which is labeled here with a yellow dot:

[[☃ image 1]]

*G. max* has the lowest percentage of identical nucleotides in its *PhyA* sequence when compared to all the other species in the table with between 68.2% and 71.9% identical nucleotides. So, *G. max* is equally related to all the other species and can be placed as the outgroup at the base of the tree.

Based on these relationships, the cladogram that is best supported is:

[[☃ image 1]]
